did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep sequential Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin lesion classification (malignant vs. benign) using the HAM10000 dataset, achieving 96.25% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a deep sequential CNN model on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. It reports specific performance metrics (96.25% accuracy), compares results against other models, and includes user testing feedback, which are characteristics of an original research study rather than a review.

title: An Intelligent Mechanism to Detect Multi-Factor Skin Cancer
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep sequential Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify malignant and benign skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model performs image preprocessing to isolate regions of interest and extract features, followed by classification into seven pigmented skin lesion classes. Evaluation was conducted using a 75/15/10 train/validation/test split, with data augmentation applied to address class imbalance. The proposed sequential CNN achieved an overall accuracy of 96.25%, outperforming baseline models such as CNN transfer learning (87.9%), VGG19 (86%), and ResNet-50 + VGG-16 (94.14%). The results demonstrate the model's potential to improve early melanoma detection and support dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign class; 6705 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Malignant class; 1113 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Benign class; 1099 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Malignant class; 514 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Precancerous/early cancerous class; 327 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Benign class; 142 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.
Benign class; 115 instances in HAM10000 dataset; used in classification task.

all_task_types.target: Seven pigmented skin lesion classes (NV, MEL, BKL, BCC, AKIEC, VASC, DF) from the HAM10000 dataset
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task using a deep sequential CNN to classify skin lesions into seven specific diagnostic categories. The model achieves 96.25% accuracy on the test set. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic labels rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A standardized public dataset of dermoscopic images primarily obtained from the ISIC archive, used as a benchmark for skin lesion classification. It contains 10,015 images across seven pigmented skin lesion classes.

use.training: Used for training the deep sequential CNN model (75% of dataset). Data augmentation was applied to mitigate class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for model validation (15% of dataset).
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation and testing (10% of dataset). No data augmentation or preprocessing was performed on test data before evaluation.
use.other: Used as a benchmark for comparison against other models (CNN transfer learning, VGG19, ResNet-50 + VGG-16, Inception v3, Vision Transformers, Entropy-NDOELM).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by comparing predicted labels against ground truth labels for each class.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC ROC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the diagnostic ability of the binary classifier systems as their discrimination threshold is varied, providing a measure of separability between classes.
To gather qualitative and quantitative feedback from 150 users (including 50 patients) regarding the usability, accuracy trust, and overall experience of the web-based diagnostic tool.
To summarize user satisfaction ratings across various features (ease of use, image upload, accuracy, quickness, etc.) on a 5-point Likert scale.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model outperformed all listed baseline models. Class-wise precision ranged from 0.91 to 1.00. F1 scores were >0.88 for all classes. Macro and Weighted averages for Precision, Recall, F1, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC ROC were all 0.96. Total support was 11,734 samples in the test set.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (trained from scratch; the paper notes that using a sequential CNN instead of pre-trained models yielded better results to avoid overtraining)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (described as 'crude input data' or 'image represented as a grid of pixel values')

layer_types: Convolutional Layers (Depth-wise separable convolutions, Dilated convolutions)
specific_techniques: Depth-wise separable convolutions to minimize computational costs
Dilated convolutions to extend receptive field
Max pooling to reduce spatial dimensions

training_details.loss_function: Sparse categorical crossentropy (compiled with Nadam); Dice Loss (used in output layer to handle class imbalance)
training_details.epochs: Up to 50 epochs (best performance at 46 epochs)
callbacks: ModelCheckpoint (saves model with best validation accuracy)
EarlyStopping (stops training if validation accuracy plateaus)
training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to training data to mitigate class imbalance

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a target size of 224 × 224 pixels for consistency and analysis
Normalization of pixel values by dividing by 255 to scale values between 0 and 1
Data resampling using RandomOverSampler to balance the dataset and address class imbalance
Reshaping input data to shape (n_samples, 28, 28, 3) to match CNN input requirements
Data augmentation applied to training data to mitigate class imbalance
No preprocessing or data augmentation performed on test data before final evaluation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to balance the dataset by over-sampling the minority class to mitigate class imbalance.
Applied to training data to mitigate class imbalance and improve model performance; specific geometric or color transform parameters were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed deep sequential Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved an overall accuracy of 96.25% in classifying seven types of pigmented skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

The proposed sequential CNN outperformed several state-of-the-art baseline models, including CNN transfer learning (87.9%), VGG19 (86%), ResNet-50 + VGG-16 (94.14%), Inception v3 (90%), Vision Transformers (92.14%), and the Entropy-NDOELM method (95.7%).

The model demonstrated high precision (ranging from 0.91 to 1.00 across classes) and F1 scores (>0.88 for all classes), indicating a balanced approach to accuracy and recall and a reduced rate of false positive diagnoses.

User testing via a web-based interface involving 150 users (including 50 patients) yielded overwhelmingly positive feedback, with 76% reporting high overall satisfaction and 82% expressing willingness to recommend the tool.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning and sequential CNNs to revolutionize melanoma detection and support dermatological diagnosis by providing fast, accurate, and non-invasive assessments.

The authors note that no previous research had demonstrated a comparative analysis of several skin cancer types using deep sequential convolutional neural networks, positioning this work as a novel contribution to the field.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., NV class comprises >67% of data) and potential selection bias from specific dermatological centers; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for performance comparisons, relying instead on descriptive accuracy metrics.

Class-wise evaluation metrics show perfect sensitivity and specificity (1.00) for several classes (NV, MEL, BCC, VASC, DF), which is unusually high and may indicate overfitting or data leakage despite claims of preventing it.

The model was trained from scratch rather than using pre-trained weights, which is a notable deviation from standard transfer learning practices in medical imaging, potentially affecting generalizability.
User satisfaction was assessed via a small, non-randomized sample of 150 users (including 50 patients) through a web interface, limiting the generalizability of the usability findings.
The paper acknowledges limitations in previous studies regarding geographical diversity and clinical practicality but does not address these biases within its own single-dataset evaluation.
No information is provided regarding the computational cost, inference time, or hardware requirements for the proposed model, limiting assessment of its clinical deployment feasibility.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (also referred to as ISIC 2018 in some contexts within the paper's literature review, but explicitly identified as HAM10000 for the experiment) for all phases of the machine learning pipeline. The dataset was split into training (75%), validation (15%), and testing (10%) sets. Data augmentation (RandomOverSampler) was applied to the training set to address class imbalance. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed; the model was trained and tested exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained, validated, and tested on the same dataset (HAM10000) using a fixed internal split. The authors note that no external validation on independent datasets was performed.

training.notes: Used for model fitting. Data augmentation via RandomOverSampler was applied to mitigate class imbalance. Images were resized to 224x224 and normalized.

validation.notes: Used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning. No data augmentation or preprocessing was performed on validation data before evaluation.

testing.notes: Used for final model evaluation. No data augmentation or preprocessing was performed on test data before final evaluation. The split was random to prevent data leakage.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The paper states the proposed sequential CNN was trained from scratch rather than using pre-trained weights, deviating from standard transfer learning practices. Baseline models like VGG19 and ResNet-50 likely used ImageNet pretraining, but this is not explicitly detailed for the proposed model.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data resampling using RandomOverSampler was used to balance the dataset by over-sampling the minority class. Specific geometric or color transform parameters for augmentation were not reported.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 75% training, 15% validation, 10% testing. The authors experimented with 70/20/10 and 60/20/20 splits but selected 75/15/10 as optimal.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set was a held-out portion of the HAM10000 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Class-wise results showed high performance. Class 0 (NV): Precision 0.99, Recall 1.00, F1 1.00. Class 1 (MEL): Precision 0.98, Recall 1.00, F1 0.99. Class 2 (BKLs): Precision 0.91, Recall 0.96, F1 0.94. Class 3 (BCC): Precision 1.00, Recall 1.00, F1 1.00. Class 4 (AKIEC): Precision 0.94, Recall 0.83, F1 0.88. Class 5 (VASCs): Precision 1.00, Recall 1.00, F1 1.00. Class 6 (DF): Precision 0.92, Recall 0.95, F1 0.93. Macro and Weighted averages for Precision, Recall, F1, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC ROC were all 0.96.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and potential selection bias from specific dermatological centers.
No external validation on independent datasets was performed, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper acknowledges limitations in previous studies regarding geographical diversity but does not address these biases within its own single-dataset evaluation.

Class-wise evaluation metrics show perfect sensitivity and specificity (1.00) for several classes, which is unusually high and may indicate overfitting or data leakage despite claims of preventing it.
information_not_reported: Exact patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient were split across train/test sets).
Specific hyperparameters for the RandomOverSampler augmentation.
Computational cost or inference time for the proposed model.
Formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for performance comparisons between models.
